Private Thought of Ito: Alright, a new project! "Free ideas" and "think outside the box" – that's music to my ears, really. Historical Game Planning is my department, but they said it's just a broad strategic direction, so I don't have to stick to just dusty old textbooks. I can definitely blend things, twist history, or even go completely wild with a historical *flavor* rather than strict accuracy. This is exactly the kind of challenge I enjoy. My brain is already buzzing with all sorts of possibilities!

Okay, so who to consult first and how to approach this task...

First things first, I'm going to spend some time just letting my mind wander, letting ideas bubble up. I'll probably grab a notepad – or maybe even just a whiteboard if I'm feeling particularly energetic – and just start scribbling down anything and everything that comes to mind. No filtering, no judgment, just pure, unadulterated brainstorming. I'll think about different historical periods, what kind of core emotional experiences could be fun, what crazy gameplay loops I've always wanted to try. Maybe a historical setting but with a complete genre twist? Or what if a well-known historical figure had a secret life? My intuitive side really thrives when I'm just playing with concepts without boundaries.

Once I've got a decent mess of ideas, a raw creative soup, that's when I'll start thinking about narrowing things down a bit, trying to find a compelling core. I'll look for themes that resonate, a unique selling point that pops, something that feels fresh and exciting.

As for who to consult first... I think I'll want to bounce some of these initial, unrefined ideas off someone who can help me see different angles, perhaps someone a bit more reflective than me, who can help ground some of my wilder flights of fancy or poke holes in something that sounds great in my head but might not work in practice. Looking at the list, Takahashi or Tanaka, with their reflective workstyles, might be good for that. They could offer a different perspective and help me refine my thoughts before I even try to solidify a concept. Watanabe is balanced, so she might be good too, offering a mix of intuition and practicality.

I wouldn't go straight to Yamamoto, our leader, right away. I want to have a somewhat cohesive, even if still rough, concept before I present it. My role is to design, and I want to bring something thought-out to the table, even if it's still in its early stages. Plus, if I can iron out some of the initial kinks with a colleague, it'll make the final pitch stronger.

So, approach: pure, unfiltered brainstorming first, then a collaborative refining session with a reflective colleague to get some early feedback on the most promising concepts, focusing on specific elements like a potential story hook or a unique gameplay mechanic. This way, I can leverage my intuitive strengths to generate ideas and then lean on a colleague's reflective nature to help me shape them into something more concrete. (formed immediately after interacting with the start of the project)
